nativeRange.habitat: Low intertidal zone and subtidal rocky reef; attaches to hard substrate. Introduced populations established in northeastern North America (invasive).

avoidWith: herbivorous fish that may consume macroalgae (e.g. tangs, rabbitfish)

imageCandidates.notes: Mosaic Macros product page image — the dispatch manifest image for this entry. May be a generic placeholder; verify before use.

summary: Dead Man's Fingers (Codium fragile) is a dark-green macroalgae with spongy, finger-like fronds that branch in a forking pattern. It prefers cooler water than most reef tanks and generally begins to show stress above 24–25°C, making it better suited to temperate or cool-water reef setups.
careNotes:
Dead Man's Fingers is a cool-water macroalgae that has been reported to show stress at typical reef temperatures above 24–25°C; aquarists keeping it in warm reef systems (26°C+) should monitor it closely. It grows as a siphonous green alga (one in which the entire thallus is a single branching cell without internal divisions) with a spongy texture — despite belonging to the order Bryopsidales it is NOT calcified, unlike closely related genera such as Halimeda or Penicillus. It typically attaches via a holdfast to hard substrate and can be propagated by fragmentation.

IMPORTANT — INVASIVE STATUS: Codium fragile subsp. fragile (and the closely related subsp. tomentosoides) is listed as invasive in the North Atlantic, where it was introduced from the Pacific (origin: Japan/NW Pacific). It has established along the eastern coasts of North America and parts of Europe, where it smothers shellfish beds and carries young oysters and mussels away — earning the trade name 'Oyster Thief.' Aquarists in Atlantic coastal regions should ensure no live material enters local waterways; never dispose of live specimens in coastal environments or drain systems connected to the sea.

Dead Man's Fingers is available from specialty online macroalgae retailers. New cuttings may arrive with hitchhiker organisms — anemones, echinoderms, annelids, and small crustaceans are all possible — so inspect new specimens carefully before introducing them to an established system.
breedingNotes: In the wild, Codium fragile reproduces sexually via separate male and female plants producing gametes; no persistent gametophyte stage is reported. In aquarium settings, fragmentation is the practical propagation method — frond fragments can establish on hard substrate if given moderate flow and lighting.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Product description confirms species identity and finger-like morphology; limited quantitative care parameters. Mosaic is the sole aquarium trade source identified for this entry.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Codium fragile
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority and ecological reference. Confirmed: Pacific origin, invasive in NE Atlantic (subsp. tomentosoides introduced to New York from Europe 1957), intertidal/subtidal rocky reef habitat, dichotomous branching, fronds to 40+ cm, 'oyster thief' common name. Used for native range, invasion history, morphology, and reproductive mode.

additional.name: Seaweed.ie — Codium fragile
additional.notes: Secondary botanical reference. Confirmed: spongy dichotomously branched light-green velvety morphology, NE Atlantic distribution (Netherlands, Britain, Ireland, France, Spain, Azores, Mediterranean). Noted genetic research questioning subspecific invasive status in some Atlantic populations.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
